summary: Implemented full step 1 of a 5-step spec to add VSCode/Cursor .code-workspace support to reportal. Step 1 (config schema + CST-based parse-merge-write for .code-workspace files) is complete and passes cargo clippy -- -D warnings. Step 2 (rep workspace subcommands) has cli_args done; command handlers not yet written. Steps 3-5 untouched. Nothing committed per user instruction — entire diff sits uncommitted in the working tree.
what_failed: Repeated guard fights consumed most of the session. The rustacean guard's POSITIONAL ARGS BAN (>1 param => Params struct) combined with SMART CONSTRUCTOR (all fields private) creates a recursive requirement that makes multi-param helper functions expensive: each needs its own wrapper type in its own file. Session stopped partway through step 2 because the remaining 25-35 files would likely exhaust context before reaching step 4 (ratatui TUI).
completed: Step 1: [workspaces.<name>] config schema with WorkspaceEntry struct + WorkspaceRegistrationBuilder
Step 1: validate_workspace_references() called from load_from_disk — fail-fast dangling-reference check
Step 1: Workspace CRUD methods (get_workspace, get_workspace_mut, add_workspace, remove_workspace, workspaces_with_names)
Step 1: workspaces_containing_repo() — reverse index required by rep edit path-change regeneration
Step 1: src/code_workspace/ module with CodeWorkspaceFile parse-merge-write via jsonc-parser CST API (no serde_json::Value held in reportal code — unknown top-level fields round-trip untouched)
Step 1: 5 new error variants (WorkspaceNotFound, WorkspaceAlreadyExists, WorkspaceHasDanglingRepo, CodeWorkspaceIoFailure, CodeWorkspaceParseFailure)
Step 1: jsonc-parser 0.32 dep added with cst feature
Step 1: 11 #[expect(dead_code, reason = ...)] markers on step-1 APIs that step 2 will consume — expect auto-errors when consumers wire up
Step 1: cargo clippy -- -D warnings passes clean

Step 2 (partial): src/cli_args/workspace_args.rs — full WorkspaceArgs + WorkspaceArgsSubcommand tree with shared extracted arg structs (WorkspaceArgsName, WorkspaceArgsRepoAlias) + payload structs (WorkspaceArgsNameOnly, WorkspaceArgsMemberEdit, WorkspaceArgsCreate) + consuming Parts return types
Step 2 (partial): cli_args/mod.rs exports updated, reportal_cli.rs has Workspace(WorkspaceArgs) variant with ws alias

in_progress.status: Blocked on rustacean_guard POSITIONAL ARGS BAN. Wrote a draft with three private helper functions (regenerate_workspace_file, resolve_workspace_member_paths, resolve_workspace_file_path) — each takes 2 params and each was flagged. Also flagged: .ok() usage on get_repo lookup (SILENT DISCARD BAN).
in_progress.next_step: Rewrite workspace_operations.rs as a WorkspaceRegenerator<'config> struct with &self methods, so each helper has 1 non-self param. The struct holds a reference to ReportalConfig. Then: workspace_listing.rs, workspace_show.rs, workspace_create.rs, workspace_delete.rs, workspace_members.rs (may split into two files if free-fn-guard hits), workspace_open.rs. Then wire reportal_commands/mod.rs + main.rs dispatch for WorkspaceArgsSubcommand variants.

remaining: Step 2: workspace_delete.rs (run_workspace_delete — does NOT remove the .code-workspace file per the args docs)
Step 2: workspace_members.rs (run_workspace_add_repo, run_workspace_remove_repo — calls regenerate after mutation)
Step 2: workspace_open.rs (run_workspace_open — runs default editor on the resolved .code-workspace path)
Step 2: reportal_commands/mod.rs exports for all workspace commands
Step 2: main.rs dispatch adding ReportalCliSubcommand::Workspace arm with match on WorkspaceArgsSubcommand
Step 2: repo_edit.rs hook — on path change, call workspaces_containing_repo() and regenerate each affected workspace file
Step 2: Consider: repo_remove.rs hook — on repo removal, either refuse if the repo is a workspace member or remove it from every workspace first
Step 3: Tag-tree or workspace-tree grouping in repo_listing.rs — per the VSCode workspace discussion, workspaces become the tree root for rep list (not tags)
Step 3: Decide: does rep list show workspaces as a grouping, and does tag grouping become a secondary filter via --tag?
Step 4: Add ratatui + crossterm deps for interactive rep list TUI
Step 4: Build interactive tree view replacing/absorbing rep dash — keybindings: arrows, enter (jump), o (open), e (edit), s (sync this repo), S (sync subtree), d (remove), / (fuzzy filter), ?  (help)
Step 4: Decide which existing commands absorb into the TUI (dash, status, list) and which stay independent
Step 5: Live git-status column in the TUI with async refresh via background thread
Cross-cutting: Remove the 11 #[expect(dead_code)] markers as step 2 wires up consumers — expect will auto-error on unfulfilled-lint-expectation, forcing their removal
context_for_next_ai: (1) DO NOT COMMIT until the full spec is done — user was explicit: 'DONT PUSH RO SHIP, FINISHT EHE NTIE SPEC'. Everything stays uncommitted in the working tree. (2) User realized mid-session that 'workspace' meant VSCode .code-workspace files, NOT an abstract grouping concept. Earlier parts of the conversation designed the wrong thing; the final design in step 1 is correct. (3) The reason jsonc-parser CST is used (not serde_json::Value flatten) is the rustacean guard's RAW JSON BAN + UNTYPED COLLECTION BAN. The CST exposes typed CstInputValue enum with no Value variant, letting reportal preserve unknown top-level .code-workspace fields (settings/extensions/launch/tasks/comments) without ever holding an untyped JSON value. See folder_parsing.rs and code_workspace_file.rs. (4) The 11 #[expect(dead_code)] markers are intentional and use expect (not allow) so they self-destruct when step 2 wires up consumers — any marker whose lint stops firing becomes a compile error, forcing its removal. (5) WorkspaceEntry stores repo ALIASES, not paths. Resolution to absolute paths happens at regeneration time via get_repo(). Moving a repo updates every containing workspace via workspaces_containing_repo() reverse index. (6) Step 3 direction changed from 'tag-tree' (before VSCode realization) to 'workspace-tree' (after). The user wants rep list grouped by workspace as the tree root. (7) Reportal's existing clippy config is strict: cargo clippy -- -D warnings must pass. pedantic=warn; multiple deny lints. Every new method needs #[must_use] and docs. (8) The rustacean guard, naming-clarity guard, doc guard, free-fn guard (max 2 per file), pub-type-guard (1 primary type per file, companions allowed if name contains primary), and repeated-union-fields guard all enforce pre-write. Plan file layouts accordingly.
key_decisions: Design B: reportal owns [workspaces.<name>] in config.toml and generates .code-workspace files from it. Not Design A (reference-only) because that fails the invariant 'if a repo moves, every workspace referencing it updates' — there's no reverse index without reportal owning the workspace definition.

Use jsonc-parser CST API (not serde_json::Value flatten) to preserve unknown .code-workspace fields. Satisfies rustacean guard's RAW JSON BAN because reportal never holds a serde_json::Value — the CST exposes typed CstInputValue.

Workspaces become the tree root in rep list (step 3), not tags. Tags become a secondary filter. Reason: workspaces answer 'what am I working on' (explicit user grouping), tags answer 'what is this repo' (classification). They are different axes.
Ratatui (step 4) absorbs rep dash into rep list as the interactive tree-with-actions surface. Single entry point for all repo operations.

Reportal writes empty display names in folder entries. Top-level fields outside folders are user-owned (preserved). Per-folder display names are reportal-owned (reset). Avoids the ownership ambiguity of preserving vs regenerating display names on path change.

#[expect(dead_code)] over #[allow(dead_code)] for self-destructing annotations. When step 2 wires up a consumer, the expect fires an unfulfilled-lint-expectation error forcing removal — no permanent hiding place.
gotchas: rustacean_guard POSITIONAL ARGS BAN: any fn with >1 param needs a Params struct, but Params structs need private fields (SMART CONSTRUCTOR) which creates recursive constructor requirements. Escape: wrap helpers in a context struct with &self + 1 arg. Used this pattern for FolderParsingContext already.

pub-type-guard allows 1 primary type per file. Companion types (name contains the primary type's substring) allowed alongside. This is why workspace_args.rs can hold WorkspaceArgs, WorkspaceArgsSubcommand, WorkspaceArgsName, WorkspaceArgsNameOnly, WorkspaceArgsMemberEdit, WorkspaceArgsMemberEditParts, WorkspaceArgsCreate, WorkspaceArgsCreateParts, WorkspaceArgsRepoAlias all in one file — they all start with WorkspaceArgs.
free-fn-guard: max 2 free functions per file. Put extras as methods on a type, or split files.

rustacean_guard MATCH MANDATE bans if-let ... else. Use plain if-let (no else) OR let-else syntax — they are not flagged as strongly. clippy's manual_let_else wants let-else, and the project enforces clippy clean.
naming-clarity hook blocks vague names like 'value', 'parser', 'config' — must be qualified (jsonc_parser, workspace_entry, etc.).

require_rust_docs hook checks doc adjacency — if you add an attribute between /// and the fn, make sure the attribute is on a single line so the hook can walk past it. Multi-line attributes break the check.
doc_markdown lint: 'VSCode' must be backticked or split as 'VS Code'. 'JSONC' too if used as an identifier-like term.
The 11 #[expect(dead_code)] markers WILL self-destruct as consumers are added. Each marker removal is forced by the compiler — do not preemptively remove them, let the compiler tell you which.

CodeWorkspaceIoFailure and CodeWorkspaceParseFailure were initially marked with #[expect(dead_code)] but it was unfulfilled because their constructors in code_workspace/* reference them — rustc considers them live via the reference even when the constructors themselves are dead. Removed. Leave them unmarked.

Step 2 command handlers need to call regenerate_workspace_file after any mutation. Create handler pattern: (1) load config, (2) mutate workspace, (3) save config, (4) call regenerate_workspace_file(&config, name). Saving the config before regenerating is important so the regeneration reads the post-mutation state.
